title: Signal engineering: intentionally design the conversion data you send to ad networks
insight: Signal engineering is the practice of intentionally designing and optimizing the conversion events and values you send back to ad networks (Meta, Google, TikTok, AppLovin) to improve campaign performance. Most advertisers use default SDK mappings without thinking about what they're actually telling the algorithm. The three main levers left for campaign optimization are: (1) creative, (2) budget/bidding, and (3) the signal — the data you share with the platform about what constitutes a valuable conversion. Creative gets 90% of the discussion, but signal engineering is an underexplored lever with significant impact.
raw_excerpt: The data signals that we send back to the ad networks to optimize campaigns from... I think this can be engineered to help improve performance... for a lot of people the conversion signal is a bit of a set and forget.
source_title: Let's Talk Ads - Signal Engineering: Buzzword or Secret Weapon? / SubClub podcast / RevenueCat Live AMA
actionable_steps: Audit what conversion events and values your app currently sends to each ad network
Check Events Manager (Meta) or Goals (Google) to verify the data platforms actually receive
Don't assume default SDK mappings are optimal — they're a starting point, not the answer
Think of signal engineering as the third optimization lever alongside creative and budget

title: Three states of signal health: broken, basic, and sophisticated — fix broken first
insight: Most advertisers fall into one of three states: (1) Broken — the events being received by the platform don't match internal data by 30-50%+, which is shockingly common even at large companies. Fix this before anything else. (2) Basic — events are passing correctly but with no optimization thought (e.g., all trials lumped together). (3) Sophisticated — engineered signals with filtered events, predicted values, and per-network customization. Always start by checking Events Manager (Meta) or Goals (Google) against your internal analytics. If discrepancy exceeds 10%, you have a broken signal problem that blocks all other optimization.
raw_excerpt: The first place I look. I don't even look at the ad account. And the first thing I'm going to check is does the amount of conversion match more or less what we have internally... in so many cases I'm seeing 30% or 50% discrepancy. This is going to be a major problem.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Before looking at ad performance, open Events Manager (Meta) or Goals (Google)
Compare platform-reported event counts (unattributed totals) against your internal analytics
If discrepancy exceeds 10%, stop all other optimization work and fix the data pipeline first
Check for common issues: duplicate events from multiple sources, misconfigured SDK mappings, default configs mapping trials+purchases+renewals all as 'purchase'

title: Qualified trials: filter out low-converting trial users by negation, not selection
insight: Instead of trying to identify your best users (positive filtering), identify characteristics of your worst-converting trial users and exclude them (negative filtering). This preserves more event volume for the algorithm while removing the biggest value destroyers. Common negative filters: users under 21-25 (high cancel rates, low purchasing power), users on very old devices, users who give low-intent onboarding answers. Negative filtering is better for scale because you remove 10-20% of low-value users rather than restricting to a tiny high-value segment that starves the campaign of signal.
raw_excerpt: Instead of looking for the best users, I say what characteristics do the worst users have? The ones that always cancel no matter what... filtering negatively rather than positively is often more efficient because you're removing the worst 10-20% rather than looking for the top 10%.
source_title: RevenueCat Live AMA - Signal Engineering / SubClub podcast
actionable_steps: Run regression analysis: which user characteristics correlate with the lowest trial-to-paid conversion?
Common negative filters: age <21, very old devices, generic/low-intent onboarding answers
Filter negatively (remove worst 10-20%) rather than positively (select top 10%)
Create a new event (e.g., 'qualified_trial') that only fires when filter conditions are met
Don't filter if you already have low event volume (<10-15/day/campaign)

title: Onboarding questions are the most powerful signal discriminator — design them for variance
insight: Onboarding survey answers create the strongest variance in user value, more than device type or demographics alone. Analyze each onboarding screen: does the answer to this question create measurable variance in trial-to-paid conversion, refund rate, or renewal rate? If a question doesn't discriminate, it's not useful for signal engineering. Some sophisticated teams design onboarding questions specifically to create this variance — not just for product personalization, but to generate discriminating signals for ad optimization. Common high-variance factors: stated goals (specific problem vs. generic browsing), user type (consumer vs. solopreneur/business), age, and anxiety/urgency level.
raw_excerpt: I'm gonna take every step in my onboarding and look, does it create variance of the revenue per trial?... at the beginning I was over-reliant on other factors. I know people who go even further in the sense that they designed the onboarding to have this information.
source_title: RevenueCat Live AMA - Signal Engineering / SubClub podcast
actionable_steps: For each onboarding question, measure: does the answer create variance in trial-to-paid, refund, or renewal?
Questions that don't discriminate are useless for signal engineering
Consider adding questions specifically designed to identify high/low value users
Common discriminators: stated goals (specific > generic), user type (business > consumer), urgency level
Use product analytics (Amplitude/Mixpanel) connected to RevenueCat to run this analysis

title: Send engineered predicted revenue, not real revenue — real values mislead ad platforms
insight: Default revenue values sent via SDKs mislead platforms: yearly subscriptions appear vastly more valuable than weekly on day 1 (but weekly users who renew 6 months may have higher LTV), free trials show zero value until conversion (days/weeks later), and renewals arrive too late to inform optimization. Instead, send predicted values based on a model: e.g., predicted value at month 13 (captures first yearly renewal). This tells the platform what users are truly worth, not what the default SDK reports. The predicted value can also be amplified: inflate high-value users and deflate low-value ones to make the algorithm's job easier. But don't go too extreme — one gaming client was shut down by an ad network for sending fake predictive values that disrupted their system.
raw_excerpt: I hate sending the real value because it comes so late with the trial that it's useless... the platform is going to overindex on yearly because all the revenue comes on day one but maybe your weekly plan has a higher LTV... I'm not trying to lie, I'm trying to fix something that is broken.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Don't send default SDK revenue values — they misrepresent subscription economics
Build a predicted value model based on plan type, user characteristics, and predicted LTV
Consider month 13 predicted value as a comparison point (captures first yearly renewal)
Amplify variance: inflate high-value user predictions, deflate low-value ones
Monitor the gap between predicted values sent and actual revenue — recalibrate regularly
Don't push amplification too far — ad networks may flag or restrict your campaigns

title: Auto-cancel trial filtering sounds obvious but has hidden pitfalls
insight: 15-30% of users cancel trial auto-renewal within minutes of starting. Filtering these out seems like a no-brainer qualified trial signal, but has two major issues: (1) Technical: the cancel status comes from a server (e.g., RevenueCat), must be relayed back to the device, and the user may have already closed the app — so the delayed event can't be sent reliably. (2) Business: some auto-cancelers are actually very high-value users who end up converting on a different subscription product later. Filtering them removes both the never-payers AND the delayed high-value converters. Test carefully before implementing, and consider whether alternative filters (age, onboarding answers) achieve the same result without the drawbacks.
raw_excerpt: I stopped using the auto cancel not for the technical reason but because I realized that a lot of people who auto cancel were actually ended up converting, but on another product... I was filtering out extremely valuable users.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance / RevenueCat AMA
actionable_steps: Don't blindly filter auto-cancelers — analyze whether they convert on other products later
Technical challenge: cancel status comes from server, user may have closed app before event fires
Consider whether simpler filters (age, onboarding answers) achieve the same quality improvement
If implementing, use a delay window (e.g., 10-30 min) and only fire the event if no cancel detected

title: Different ad networks respond differently to the same signal — customize per platform
insight: Meta's algorithm is the most sophisticated at interpreting value signals but also the most reactive (changes can cause big disruptions). Google has more inertia — slower to react. TikTok is more primitive in signal interpretation. AppLovin handles value optimization very well. Apple Search Ads sees nothing beyond the click — no signal engineering possible. In practice, apply universal filters (e.g., exclude sub-21 trials) to all networks, but customize when you detect platform-specific problems. Example: one network may overindex on young audiences where trial-to-paid crashes — filter specifically for that network. Don't over-engineer for every network simultaneously.
raw_excerpt: Different networks behave differently and they interpret the signal differently... TikTok is a little bit more primitive, the one from Meta is very sophisticated... sometimes you can personalize but don't try to optimize the signal for every single network all the time.
source_title: Let's Talk Ads - Signal Engineering: Buzzword or Secret Weapon?
actionable_steps: Apply universal quality filters (e.g., age exclusions) to all networks simultaneously
Customize signals per-network only when you detect a specific problem (e.g., one network's trial-to-paid crashes)
Meta: most sophisticated but most reactive to signal changes — be careful with big changes
Google Android: value optimization (tROAS) is very powerful; iOS value optimization is poor
AppLovin: strong value optimization capabilities
Apple Search Ads: no signal engineering possible — bid on keywords only

title: Value amplification: expand variance for subscription apps, compress it for gaming whales
insight: For subscription apps where revenue variance is naturally low (users pay similar amounts), amplify the difference: tell the platform a $5 user is worth $3 and a $50 user is worth $100. This makes the algorithm's optimization task clearer. For gaming apps with extreme variance (whales spending $500-1000), do the opposite: compress the variance by capping high values (send $50 instead of $500) to prevent one whale conversion from satisfying the algorithm's daily goals and causing it to deliver garbage traffic for the rest of the day. The key insight: subscription apps need more variance in their signal, gaming apps need less.
raw_excerpt: I'm trying to gear towards the most valuable users by putting even more value... someone from gaming told me 'I do the opposite, I lower the highest valued users'... in subscription I expand the variance, in gaming they reduce it because one whale can completely disrupt deliveries.
source_title: Let's Talk Ads - Signal Engineering: Buzzword or Secret Weapon?
actionable_steps: For subscription apps: amplify value differences — inflate high-value, deflate low-value predictions
For gaming/IAP apps with whales: compress variance — cap whale values to prevent algorithm disruption
Accept that faked values make platform-reported ROAS unreadable — measure true ROAS in your own analytics
Start with moderate amplification (2-3x) before going extreme

title: Conversion events must happen within 24 hours — anything later is nearly useless for optimization
insight: Ad platforms optimize effectively only on signals that arrive within the first 24 hours. A 7-day or 14-day trial conversion arriving on day 7 is almost useless for the algorithm's real-time optimization. If your trial is 7 days, find a proxy event within the first day: e.g., completed 3 activities in 2 hours, opened the payment form, completed onboarding with high-intent answers. You can delay firing the event by a few hours (to verify quality indicators) but don't wait beyond 24 hours. Reducing trial length from 7 to 3 days doesn't help much — if it's not day 1, it's already suboptimal.
raw_excerpt: I'm entirely convinced that whatever comes after 24 hours is useless for the platform to optimize towards... you want to find something that happens on the first day... you can wait a few hours and then send it, that's it.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Find a conversion proxy that happens within 24 hours of install, ideally within hours
For apps with 7-day trials: use first-day engagement events as proxy (e.g., 3 activities completed)
You can delay event firing by 1-2 hours to verify quality (e.g., wait to see if they engage)
Reducing trial from 7 to 3 days doesn't meaningfully help — day 1 signal is what matters

title: Minimum 10 events/day/campaign — below this, go higher in funnel instead of filtering
insight: Each campaign needs roughly 10+ conversion events per day (some say 50-100/week) for the algorithm to optimize effectively. If you're below this threshold, you cannot afford to filter events — go higher in the funnel instead (e.g., optimize for all trials instead of qualified trials). As your budget and volume grow, you earn the right to filter more aggressively. At scale (40+ events/day), the upside of sending 30 highly filtered events vastly outweighs sending 300 unfiltered ones. This creates a natural progression: start broad, then narrow as volume allows.
raw_excerpt: If you're not sending at least 10 per day the campaign is very likely to be unstable... once you get there, there's very little upside to sending 300 every day. And there's a lot of upside of sending only 30 that are super filtered.
actionable_steps: Minimum 10 events/day/campaign for stable optimization
Below threshold: go higher in funnel (all trials) rather than filter
Above threshold (40+/day): filtering becomes highly valuable — quality beats quantity
As budget grows, progressively tighten filters to improve signal quality
Revisit signal strategy every 6 months as volume and product evolve

title: Build flexible signal infrastructure: API-driven values, not hardcoded multipliers
insight: If you're going to do signal engineering, avoid hardcoding value multipliers into your app code. Every plan change, pricing update, or currency fluctuation requires a code change and app release. Instead, build an API endpoint that the app queries at event time: given these user parameters, what value should I send? This lets you dynamically update values without touching code. Especially critical for apps operating in countries with volatile currencies (e.g., Turkey). The implementation is technically simple (one API call returning a value) but requires clear communication between marketing and engineering teams.
raw_excerpt: If I know I'm going to do a lot of signal engineering, what I'm doing is there's an API call and say, I've got all these parameters, what's the value right now? Oh, 56 and next week 68. I'm not going to have anyone touch the code.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Build a server-side API that returns the value to send for a given set of user parameters
Avoid hardcoding value multipliers in the app — every change requires a release
Critical for apps in volatile-currency markets (Turkey, Argentina, etc.)
The API call is technically simple — main challenge is marketer-engineer communication

title: Don't mix signal sources carelessly — pick one per platform or set up deduplication properly
insight: Sending events from both the platform SDK (e.g., Meta SDK) and an MMP simultaneously frequently causes duplicate or mismatched events that corrupt campaign optimization. If you mix sources, you must configure deduplication correctly (Meta, Google handle this differently). Safest approach for beginners: pick one source per platform. Meta SDK is free and works well for Meta-only campaigns. Firebase is best for Google. MMPs are needed when running multiple networks. SKAN runs in parallel and generally doesn't conflict with other sources. The Meta SDK and MMPs have different match rates — test which attributes better for your specific setup.
raw_excerpt: Very often I see people having problems because they're sending from different sources and they mix up... the deduplication is not necessarily perfect... if you're not super sophisticated, pick one of the two, get started from there.
actionable_steps: Pick one signal source per platform when starting out (Meta SDK for Meta, Firebase for Google)
If using both SDK and MMP, configure deduplication carefully — test thoroughly
SKAN runs in parallel and generally doesn't conflict with SDK/MMP events
Test whether Meta SDK or MMP attributes more efficiently for your specific setup
Fewer intermediaries = fewer breakpoints = better signal quality

title: Signal engineering is an advanced lever — prioritize creative production and fix data first
insight: Creative iteration has the most immediate, visible, and largest impact on campaign performance — 80-90% of optimization effort should go there. Signal engineering is for when: (1) your data is broken (fix immediately), (2) you see a specific down-funnel problem (e.g., trial-to-paid crashes on one network), or (3) you're at scale and have exhausted creative gains. Don't attempt sophisticated signal engineering if you're spending <$1K/day, have fewer than 5 trials/day, or don't have an engineer available. Most people have much bigger gains available in creative production, pricing, and onboarding optimization. Signal changes are infrequent (monthly/quarterly), not weekly iterations.
raw_excerpt: 80% or 90% of the effort should be on creative... if you're just starting and you're spending 1k a day on Facebook don't do sophisticated signal engineering... the real battle is on the creative side, it's clear.
source_title: Let's Talk Ads - Signal Engineering / SubClub podcast
actionable_steps: Prioritize: (1) fix broken data, (2) creative production/iteration, (3) signal engineering
Don't attempt sophisticated signal work if spending <$1K/day or <5 trials/day
Signal changes are infrequent (monthly/quarterly) — not weekly iterations like creative
If trial-to-paid from paid traffic is fine, don't bother qualifying trials — focus on creative
Triple investment in creative before investing in signal engineering

title: Use standard Meta events, not custom events — and send all events to all platforms for reporting
insight: Always optimize campaigns on standard platform events (purchase, subscribe, complete_registration) because the platforms have billions of data points for these. Custom events lack this context. Send 100% of your events (including organic) to all platforms — this gives the algorithm more matching data and gives you better reporting. Don't restrict which events each network sees. More events for analytics is good (track key funnel steps), but don't treat the ad platform as your product analytics tool — 5-10 key events is plenty.
raw_excerpt: You shouldn't restrict. You should be careful and not necessarily trust what the platform is telling you, but at least you should give it the opportunity to attribute... someone not converting is a signal they can base decisions on. The more there is, the better.
actionable_steps: Send 100% of events (including organic) to all ad platforms — don't restrict by media source
Optimize campaigns on standard events (purchase, subscribe, complete_registration)
Send additional events for reporting (5-10 key funnel steps) but don't over-track
The platform you optimize for receives your optimization event; others get everything for reporting

title: Meta's bid modifiers (value-based rules) complement but don't replace signal filtering
insight: Meta now offers bid modifiers (called 'value-based rules') that let you tell the platform certain user segments are more or less valuable (e.g., users on Google Pixel get +20%, old Android devices get deprioritized). This is complementary to qualified trial filtering — ideally use both approaches simultaneously. Bid modifiers avoid the binary problem of completely excluding users, letting you weight them instead. However, bid modifiers aren't available on all platforms, so signal engineering (filtering events, engineering values) remains necessary for cross-platform consistency.
raw_excerpt: Facebook has finally put out of beta the bit modifier... I could tell Facebook when you're seeing one of those Google Pixel assign 20% plus... but I don't want to exclude everybody below 25 because I'm limiting my options... I'd prefer to hammer from both sides.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Use Meta's value-based rules (bid modifiers) to weight user segments by value
Combine bid modifiers with qualified trial filtering for maximum effect
Bid modifiers avoid binary exclusion — weight segments instead of cutting them entirely
Still do signal engineering for cross-platform consistency (not all networks have bid modifiers)

title: Revisit signal engineering assumptions every 6 months — hardcoded values drift from reality
insight: Predicted values and filtering rules drift from reality as products change: new plans are introduced, prices change, user mix shifts across channels, currencies fluctuate, and promotional periods distort data. Monitor the gap between values you're sending to platforms and actual revenue continuously. Don't just look at total deviation — segment by country, platform, and user type to catch localized drift. Recalibrate at least every 6 months. One team hardcoded multipliers that made sense 18 months ago but had become completely unrepresentative by the time it was discovered.
raw_excerpt: We keep revisiting the value because plans change, monetization changes, the mix of channels is different... I made this mistake several times, you create useless fluctuation on very small samples... don't look at this gap only in total.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Monitor gap between sent values and actual revenue at least monthly
Segment the gap analysis by country, platform, and user type — total can hide localized drift
Recalibrate value multipliers and filtering rules at least every 6 months
After monetization changes (new plans, pricing updates), recalibrate immediately

title: Have multiple optimization events ready as backup — don't put all eggs in one basket
insight: Even if one optimization event works perfectly now, it may stop working. Have alternative events mapped and ready in your ad platforms so you can switch quickly. Run parallel campaigns optimizing for different events (e.g., one for value optimization, one for qualified trials) to access different audience segments and de-risk against platform changes. When one approach breaks — and it will — you need optionality. One client had massive success scaling on a single event type for years, then it broke and they had no alternative, causing a major revenue crisis.
raw_excerpt: One of the reasons to have your signals properly handled is to have optionality... I had a case where they put all their eggs in one basket and it worked great for a long time but when it broke they had no alternative.
source_title: SubClub podcast - Strategic Data Filtering for Better Ad Performance
actionable_steps: Map multiple optimization events in every ad platform (even if you only use one actively)
Run at least one parallel campaign optimizing for a different event type
Different event types access different audience segments — useful for scaling
When primary optimization breaks, have alternatives ready to switch within hours, not weeks

title: Value optimization campaigns: powerful but volatile — best on Google Android and AppLovin
insight: Value optimization campaigns (sending revenue/predicted LTV instead of binary events) are extremely powerful for scale and returns but come with higher volatility and require more data. They work best on Google Android (UAC tROAS) and AppLovin. On Meta, value campaigns are less stable. On Google iOS, they're terrible (Google even removed them temporarily). Value campaigns need more events than event optimization (because the algorithm needs to differentiate between value tiers, not just binary conversions). Subscription apps struggle because revenue variance is naturally low compared to gaming/e-commerce — you may need to artificially create variance through predicted values.
raw_excerpt: Value optimized campaigns require more data, obviously... they have more volatility, they go sideways very fast... on Google Android they're extremely powerful. On Google iOS they're terrible, don't even try.
source_title: RevenueCat Live AMA - Signal Engineering / SubClub podcast
actionable_steps: Google Android tROAS: very powerful for value optimization — prioritize here
AppLovin: also handles value optimization very well
Meta: value campaigns are less stable — consider event optimization as primary
Google iOS: avoid value optimization (historically terrible, recently re-enabled but unproven)
Subscription apps need to artificially create revenue variance for value campaigns to work
